* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mishima Kofun Group]][[ja:三島古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Ibaraki|WD=Q502403}}; {{ill|Takatsuki|WD=Q335423}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17211948]] Wikidata name: Mishima Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=May 2013}} '''Mishima Kofun Group''' (三島古墳群, Mishima Kofun-gun, also known as Mishimano Kofun Group) refers to the collective name for ancient tombs ('''kofun''') and archaeological sites located along the '''northern bank of the Yodo River''', spanning the '''Hio River Basin''' to the '''Ibaraki River Basin''' in '''Takatsuki City''' and '''Ibaraki City''', Osaka Prefecture (formerly '''Mishima District''').
== Overview == [[File:Yodo River and Yamato River Basins.svg|300px|thumb|right|The location of the Mishima Kofun Group in relation to other kofun groups]] Located on the '''right bank of the Yodo River''', the kofun group extends from the mountain slopes of the '''Hokusetsu Mountain Range''' to its foothills and consists of over '''500 kofun''' of varying sizes. This region was the center of ancient Mishima Province and contains representative Kofuns from the '''early to final periods of the Kofun era'''. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mishima Kofun Group]][[ja:三島古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Ibaraki|WD=Q502403}}; {{ill|Takatsuki|WD=Q335423}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211948]] Wikidata name: Mishima Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=May 2013}} '''Mishima Kofun Group''' (三島古墳群, Mishima Kofun-gun, also known as Mishimano Kofun Group) refers to the collective name for ancient tombs ('''kofun''') and archaeological sites located along the '''northern bank of the Yodo River''', spanning the '''Hio River Basin''' to the '''Ibaraki River Basin''' in '''Takatsuki City''' and '''Ibaraki City''', Osaka Prefecture (formerly '''Mishima District''').
== Overview == [[File:Yodo River and Yamato River Basins.svg|300px|thumb|right|The location of the Mishima Kofun Group in relation to other kofun groups]] Located on the '''right bank of the Yodo River''', the kofun group extends from the mountain slopes of the '''Hokusetsu Mountain Range''' to its foothills and consists of over '''500 kofun''' of varying sizes. This region was the center of ancient Mishima Province and contains representative Kofuns from the '''early to final periods of the Kofun era'''. Alongside the [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]] near '''Habikino''' and '''Fujiidera''', and the [[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Group]] in '''Sakai''', the Mishima Kofun Group is one of the largest Kofun clusters in Japan.
== Major Kofun == * '''Amanomiya Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''mid-3rd century''', marking the earliest phase of the Kofun period. The oldest kofun in the Mishima Kofun Group.
* '''Okamotoyama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered the first '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun ('''zenpō-kōen-fun''') measuring approximately '''120 meters''' in total length and '''70 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.
<gallery> File:Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun]] File:Oda Chausuyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Ōta Chausuyama Kofun]] File:Imashirozuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|[[Imashirozuka Kofun]] File:Abuyama Kofun, boshitsu.jpg|[[Abuyama Kofun]] </gallery>

* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mishima Kofun Group]][[ja:三島古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Ibaraki|WD=Q502403}}; {{ill|Takatsuki|WD=Q335423}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211948]] Wikidata name: Mishima Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=May 2013}} '''Mishima Kofun Group''' (三島古墳群, Mishima Kofun-gun, also known as Mishimano Kofun Group) refers to the collective name for ancient tombs ('''kofun''') and archaeological sites located along the '''northern bank of the Yodo River''', spanning the '''Hio River Basin''' to the '''Ibaraki River Basin''' in '''Takatsuki City''' and '''Ibaraki City''', Osaka Prefecture (formerly '''Mishima District''').
== Overview == [[File:Yodo River and Yamato River Basins.svg|300px|thumb|right|The location of the Mishima Kofun Group in relation to other kofun groups]] Located on the '''right bank of the Yodo River''', the kofun group extends from the mountain slopes of the '''Hokusetsu Mountain Range''' to its foothills and consists of over '''500 kofun''' of varying sizes. This region was the center of ancient Mishima Province and contains representative Kofuns from the '''early to final periods of the Kofun era'''. Alongside the [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]] near '''Habikino''' and '''Fujiidera''', and the [[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Group]] in '''Sakai''', the Mishima Kofun Group is one of the largest Kofun clusters in Japan.

* '''Okamotoyama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered the first '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. * '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.

* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.

revision.comment: Bot: reorganize categories (alphabetical, deduplicated, moved to bottom)
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Mishima Kofun Group]][[ja:三島古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Mishima Kofun Group]][[ja:三島古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mishima Kofun Group]][[ja:三島古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Ibaraki|WD=Q502403}}; {{ill|Takatsuki|WD=Q335423}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17211948]] Wikidata name: Mishima Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=May 2013}} '''Mishima Kofun Group''' (三島古墳群, Mishima Kofun-gun, also known as Mishimano Kofun Group) refers to the collective name for ancient tombs ('''kofun''') and archaeological sites located along the '''northern bank of the Yodo River''', spanning the '''Hio River Basin''' to the '''Ibaraki River Basin''' in '''Takatsuki City''' and '''Ibaraki City''', Osaka Prefecture (formerly '''Mishima District''').
== Overview == [[File:Yodo River and Yamato River Basins.svg|300px|thumb|right|The location of the Mishima Kofun Group in relation to other kofun groups]] Located on the '''right bank of the Yodo River''', the kofun group extends from the mountain slopes of the '''Hokusetsu Mountain Range''' to its foothills and consists of over '''500 kofun''' of varying sizes. This region was the center of ancient Mishima Province and contains representative Kofuns from the '''early to final periods of the Kofun era'''. Alongside the [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]] near '''Habikino''' and '''Fujiidera''', and the [[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Group]] in '''Sakai''', the Mishima Kofun Group is one of the largest Kofun clusters in Japan.
== Major Kofun == * '''Amanomiya Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''mid-3rd century''', marking the earliest phase of the Kofun period. The oldest kofun in the Mishima Kofun Group.
* '''Okamotoyama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered the first '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun ('''zenpō-kōen-fun''') measuring approximately '''120 meters''' in total length and '''70 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.
<gallery> File:Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun]] File:Oda Chausuyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Ōta Chausuyama Kofun]] File:Imashirozuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|[[Imashirozuka Kofun]] File:Abuyama Kofun, boshitsu.jpg|[[Abuyama Kofun]] </gallery>

== Major Kofun == * '''Amanomiya Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''mid-3rd century''', marking the earliest phase of the Kofun period. The oldest kofun in the Mishima Kofun Group.
* '''Okamotoyama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered the first '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun ('''zenpō-kōen-fun''') measuring approximately '''120 meters''' in total length and '''70 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.
<gallery> File:Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun]] File:Oda Chausuyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Ōta Chausuyama Kofun]] File:Imashirozuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|[[Imashirozuka Kofun]] File:Abuyama Kofun, boshitsu.jpg|[[Abuyama Kofun]] </gallery>

Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.

{{Unreferenced|date=May 2013}} '''Mishima Kofun Group''' (三島古墳群, Mishima Kofun-gun, also known as Mishimano Kofun Group) refers to the collective name for ancient tombs ('''kofun''') and archaeological sites located along the '''northern bank of the Yodo River''', spanning the '''Hio River Basin''' to the '''Ibaraki River Basin''' in '''Takatsuki City''' and '''Ibaraki City''', Osaka Prefecture (formerly '''Mishima District''').
== Overview == [[File:Yodo River and Yamato River Basins.svg|300px|thumb|right|The location of the Mishima Kofun Group in relation to other kofun groups]] Located on the '''right bank of the Yodo River''', the kofun group extends from the mountain slopes of the '''Hokusetsu Mountain Range''' to its foothills and consists of over '''500 kofun''' of varying sizes. This region was the center of ancient Mishima Province and contains representative Kofuns from the '''early to final periods of the Kofun era'''. Alongside the [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]] near '''Habikino''' and '''Fujiidera''', and the [[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Group]] in '''Sakai''', the Mishima Kofun Group is one of the largest Kofun clusters in Japan.
== Major Kofun == * '''Amanomiya Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''mid-3rd century''', marking the earliest phase of the Kofun period. The oldest kofun in the Mishima Kofun Group.
* '''Okamotoyama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered the first '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun ('''zenpō-kōen-fun''') measuring approximately '''120 meters''' in total length and '''70 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.

* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.

{{Unreferenced|date=May 2013}} '''Mishima Kofun Group''' (三島古墳群, Mishima Kofun-gun, also known as Mishimano Kofun Group) refers to the collective name for ancient tombs ('''kofun''') and archaeological sites located along the '''northern bank of the Yodo River''', spanning the '''Hio River Basin''' to the '''Ibaraki River Basin''' in '''Takatsuki City''' and '''Ibaraki City''', Osaka Prefecture (formerly '''Mishima District''').
== Overview == [[File:Yodo River and Yamato River Basins.svg|300px|thumb|right|The location of the Mishima Kofun Group in relation to other kofun groups]] Located on the '''right bank of the Yodo River''', the kofun group extends from the mountain slopes of the '''Hokusetsu Mountain Range''' to its foothills and consists of over '''500 kofun''' of varying sizes. This region was the center of ancient Mishima Province and contains representative Kofuns from the '''early to final periods of the Kofun era'''. Alongside the [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]] near '''Habikino''' and '''Fujiidera''', and the [[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Group]] in '''Sakai''', the Mishima Kofun Group is one of the largest Kofun clusters in Japan.
== Major Kofun == * '''Amanomiya Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''mid-3rd century''', marking the earliest phase of the Kofun period. The oldest kofun in the Mishima Kofun Group.
* '''Okamotoyama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered the first '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun ('''zenpō-kōen-fun''') measuring approximately '''120 meters''' in total length and '''70 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.
<gallery> File:Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun]] File:Oda Chausuyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Ōta Chausuyama Kofun]] File:Imashirozuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|[[Imashirozuka Kofun]] File:Abuyama Kofun, boshitsu.jpg|[[Abuyama Kofun]] </gallery>

{{Unreferenced|date=May 2013}} '''Mishima Kofun Group''' (三島古墳群, Mishima Kofun-gun, also known as Mishimano Kofun Group) refers to the collective name for ancient tombs ('''kofun''') and archaeological sites located along the '''northern bank of the Yodo River''', spanning the '''Hio River Basin''' to the '''Ibaraki River Basin''' in '''Takatsuki City''' and '''Ibaraki City''', Osaka Prefecture (formerly '''Mishima District''').
== Overview == [[File:Yodo River and Yamato River Basins.svg|300px|thumb|right|The location of the Mishima Kofun Group in relation to other kofun groups]] Located on the '''right bank of the Yodo River''', the kofun group extends from the mountain slopes of the '''Hokusetsu Mountain Range''' to its foothills and consists of over '''500 kofun''' of varying sizes. This region was the center of ancient Mishima Province and contains representative Kofuns from the '''early to final periods of the Kofun era'''. Alongside the [[Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Group]] near '''Habikino''' and '''Fujiidera''', and the [[Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Group]] in '''Sakai''', the Mishima Kofun Group is one of the largest Kofun clusters in Japan.
== Major Kofun == * '''Amanomiya Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''mid-3rd century''', marking the earliest phase of the Kofun period. The oldest kofun in the Mishima Kofun Group.
* '''Okamotoyama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered the first '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun ('''zenpō-kōen-fun''') measuring approximately '''120 meters''' in total length and '''70 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Benten-yama Kofun''' : A kofun from the '''late 3rd to early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Thought to be the '''royal tomb''' of the rulers of Mishima. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''100 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''70 meters'''.
* '''Tokeiyama Kofun''' (闘鶏山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''86.4 meters''' and a rear mound diameter of '''60 meters'''.
* '''Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun''' (郡家車塚古墳) : A kofun from the '''late 4th century''', an early Kofun period tomb. Considered a '''royal tomb''' of the Mishima region. A keyhole-shaped kofun measuring approximately '''85.6 meters''' in total length and '''51.3 meters''' in diameter at the rear mound.
* '''Ōta Chausuyama Kofun''' (太田茶臼山古墳) – *Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai* : A kofun from the '''mid-to-late 5th century''', a mid-Kofun period tomb. A keyhole-shaped kofun with a total length of '''250 meters''' and a total width of approximately '''150 meters'''.
* '''Imashirozuka Kofun''' (今城塚古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''early 6th century''', a late Kofun period tomb. The '''largest Kofun in the Yodo River basin''', measuring approximately '''350 meters''' in total length and '''340 meters''' in total width.
* '''Tsukahara Kofun Group''' (塚原古墳群) : A kofun group dating from the '''mid-6th to mid-7th century'''. Previously contained '''15 subgroups''' with '''about 110 kofun''', but only '''40 kofun''' remain today. Known as '''Tsukahara Hachijūzuka''', it was introduced to the world by '''William Gowland''', becoming one of the earliest kofun sites in the history of Japanese archaeology.
* '''Tsukawaki Kofun Group''' : A kofun group from the '''late 6th to 7th century''', consisting of approximately '''50 kofun'''.
* '''Aman Kofun Group''' : A '''clustered kofun site''' from the '''late 6th to 7th century'''. Over '''40 kofun''' with '''yokoana-shiki sekishitsu''' (horizontal stone chamber burial sites) have been identified.
* '''Abuyama Kofun''' (阿武山古墳) – *National Historic Site* : A kofun from the '''late 7th century''', in the final phase of the Kofun period. A circular kofun ('''empun''') with no mound, measuring '''82 meters''' in diameter. Thought to be the '''tomb of Fujiwara no Kamatari'''.
<gallery> File:Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Gunke Kurumazuka Kofun]] File:Oda Chausuyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|[[Ōta Chausuyama Kofun]] File:Imashirozuka Kofun, zenkei-2.jpg|[[Imashirozuka Kofun]] File:Abuyama Kofun, boshitsu.jpg|[[Abuyama Kofun]] </gallery>
